risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization to the three treatment groups was stratified by participant’s baseline chronic pain grade and recruitment source ... and with blocking to ensure that the groups would be (nearly) balanced across the study period and within each stratum. Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6, and were chosen randomly with 2/3 and 1/3 probability, respectively.... The randomization list was prepared using the 'sample' function of the S-PLUS® statistical software." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with stratification and random block sizes, which is a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization." Comment: The paper explicitly states that assignments were concealed from personnel with participant contact until randomization, using sealed, sequentially numbered envelopes prepared by a study assistant not involved in screening/randomization. This is an explicit concealment mechanism preventing foreknowledge of assignment; although 'opaque' is not stated, the article directly says assignments were concealed to study personnel until envelope opening, which clearly indicates concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Potential participants were told that the study involved randomization to COCT or to one of two different versions of SMT." and "At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." and "Women randomized to the COCT group were seen by an Advanced Registered Nurse Practitioner..." and "The SMT and TSMT interventions consisted of two 1.5-hour in-person sessions... delivered by dental hygienists..." Comment: Participants and treatment personnel were clearly aware of at least the major allocation distinction (medication/COCT versus self-management therapy delivered by dental hygienists); only the difference between the two self-management variants was partially masked to participants. Because the main study outcomes were subjective and self-reported (pain intensity, activity interference, pain quality, depression, treatment helpfulness), knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes. This is positive evidence of incomplete blinding with outcomes susceptible to performance bias.

Quote: "At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." and "Participants completed the study measures at the time of study enrollment (baseline, prior to randomization) and at 6 and 12 months after randomization." and outcome measures included "The Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS) was used to assess pain intensity and interference..." "The Short Form McGill Pain Questionnaire (SF MPQ) was used to assess sensory and affective dimensions of the pain experience." "The 21-item Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) was used to assess depressive symptom severity." "At the 6-month assessment, study participants rated improvement in their facial pain... [and] overall satisfaction with the treatment." Comment: The outcome assessor for the main outcomes was primarily the patient (self-report questionnaires/ratings). Although participants were partially masked between SMT and TSMT regarding the menstrual-cycle focus, they could not be blinded to receiving behavioral self-management versus oral contraceptive therapy. Because the study included multiple subjective self-reported outcomes (pain intensity, activity interference, pain quality, depression, treatment helpfulness/satisfaction), knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Applying the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded/likely unblinded patient-reported subjective outcomes makes this domain high risk overall.

Quote: "Among the 191 women who were randomized, 20 were withdrawn from the study (15 of whom were randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications). In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment." and "The primary analyses comparing the three study groups were intent-to-treat analyses, which included all randomized participants for whom follow-up data (either at 6 months or 12 months) were available" and "In sum, among 74 women randomized to the COCT arm of the study, only 25 (34%) completed the entire COC protocol. In contrast, both of the in-person self-management treatment sessions were completed by 46 (77%) of the 60 women randomized to SMT and 47 (82%) of the 57 women randomized to TSMT." Comment: Post-randomization exclusions/losses were substantial. Twenty of 191 randomized participants were withdrawn after randomization (mostly in COCT), leaving an analysis sample of 147 and excluding 24 with no follow-up data; thus 44/191 (about 23%) randomized participants did not contribute to the main analysed sample, with clear imbalance in post-randomization withdrawals toward COCT. In addition, treatment noncompletion was extreme in COCT (only 34% completed protocol, many never started or stopped for side effects), and the main analysis excluded randomized participants with no follow-up rather than true ITT. These reported numbers and reasons indicate a real risk that missing outcome data and post-randomization exclusions biased outcome estimates, especially against the COCT arm.
